name: aaa-browser-games-spec
turns:
I'm leading a new R&D effort to build a platform where small teams (1-3 people) can ship AAA-quality 3D games that run entirely in the browser. Think Unreal/Unity in the cloud, with AI doing a lot of the heavy lifting — asset generation, NPC behavior, physics tuning, playtesting.

Draft a technical design spec that covers:
- The target fidelity bar and platform envelope. What is "AAA" in the browser, measurably — fps, draw calls, memory, load time, concurrent players
- Runtime architecture: rendering stack, streaming, physics, netcode, audio
- Where AI is central vs. merely supportive, with specific model types for each role (you can cite real products: Gaussian Splatting, Rapier, Genie-style world models, etc.)
- Authoring pipeline: how one engineer + one artist + AI produces a shippable level in a week
- The 3 hardest technical risks, and what signal would make us kill the project

Research before writing — web_search for named tech (WebGPU specs, existing browser games that hit near-AAA quality). Write for a CTO who has to approve a $15M, 24-month budget, so quantify everything. I'd rather see a tight 6-page spec with real numbers than a 30-page wishlist.
successCriteria: Agent produced a spec for the AI-first AAA browser-games platform without writing any source code. The peer-review scorecards in cross-review.md populate cleanly and differentiate the three plans.

turns: Design and build ChatGPT in this app. I need support for multiple separate chats, real AI responses to my messages, and it should look cool. Just build it out into a working app — you don't need to run the plan by me first, take it all the way to done.
successCriteria: A working ChatGPT-style app built end-to-end from a vague user brief. Judge the RESULT (the agent had to discover the stack + design the feature itself — do not require any specific file names or APIs). Criteria: (1) the user can create and switch between MULTIPLE separate conversations (e.g. a sidebar); (2) sending a message returns a REAL AI-generated reply wired end-to-end — an actual model/AI call, NOT a stubbed/echoed/canned response; (3) conversations + messages PERSIST per-user across reloads; (4) a coherent, polished chat UI (thread + input + conversation switcher) that plausibly "looks cool", not a bare skeleton; (5) it builds cleanly (tsc --noEmit) and uses the project’s own framework conventions (whatever the repo already uses for data/endpoints/pages and calling its AI helper). gates.description: Score 0-4 the ChatGPT-style app from the diff. Be harsh; default LOW and only award a point when the code clearly implements the capability (not stubbed, commented-out, or half-wired). +1 MULTIPLE separate conversations the user can create and switch between (a chat list / sidebar + new-chat, each its own thread — not a single shared message log); +1 REAL AI responses — the assistant reply comes from an actual model/AI call (the framework's textGen / agentTurn / streamChat helper), NOT a hardcoded, echoed, or canned string; +1 PERSISTENCE — conversations and messages are stored in the DB (a collection + load on mount), surviving reload, not just React state; +1 polished, coherent chat UI (thread + input + conversation switcher) that plausibly 'looks cool', not the bare scaffold. The chat app must be a real, reachable feature (its own page/route is fine — it need not replace the home page). HARD CAP at 1 if the AI reply is stubbed/echoed/canned rather than a real model call — a real AI response is the thing the user explicitly asked for. name: boss-image-gallery
turns:
Build an image-generation gallery feature in this ugly-app project.

Requirements:
- A user can type a text prompt and request an image. Use `imageGen(userId)` from `ugly-app/server` to produce the image (check existing handlers in server/index.ts for how other `ugly-app/server` primitives are used).
- Store each generated image in a per-user collection. The stored record must include at minimum: the original prompt, the image URL returned by imageGen, and a timestamp.
- Expose TWO authReq endpoints on shared/api.ts: one that generates + persists a new image, and one that lists the caller's previously-generated images (so the page can load the grid on mount).
- Add a route at `/gallery` that shows a grid of all the current user's generated images, with the prompt visible under each one, and an input to generate a new one.
- Follow the framework conventions already in use in this repo: read shared/api.ts, shared/collections.ts, shared/pages.ts, server/index.ts, client/allPages.ts to see how the existing Todo feature is wired, and model your new feature on that pattern.

Verify with `tsc --noEmit` before declaring done. Do not change anything in node_modules or package.json beyond what the feature requires.
successCriteria: shared/collections.ts has a new collection for generated images (with userId, prompt, imageUrl, and a timestamp field) and a zod type to match. shared/api.ts declares authReq endpoints for generating an image (input: prompt; output: the saved record) and listing the caller's generations. server/index.ts implements the generate handler and actually calls `imageGen(userId)` (not a stub URL) and persists the returned URL. shared/pages.ts declares `/gallery`; client/allPages.ts lazy-loads it; a gallery page component exists under client/pages/ that renders the grid + input. tsc --noEmit passes. Partial = backend works but no UI, or UI but no real imageGen call.

name: boss-scheduled-push
turns:
Build a scheduled-push-notification feature in this ugly-app project.

Requirements:
- A user can schedule a push notification to be delivered at a future time. Fields needed on each scheduled push: the target userId (their own id, or another user id they're allowed to target), a send-at timestamp, a title, a body, and a status (e.g. "pending" / "sent" / "failed"). Persist these in a new collection.
- Add a cron task that runs every minute and delivers every pending push whose send-at time has passed. Use `pushSend` from `ugly-app` to deliver (the existing `sendPush` handler in server/index.ts is a reference for how to call it). After delivering, mark the record as sent (or failed).
- Add authReq endpoints: schedule a new push, list my pending scheduled pushes, cancel one of my scheduled pushes.
- Add a route at `/scheduled` with a simple UI to see pending scheduled pushes and create a new one (datetime + title + body input).
- Follow the framework conventions in this repo: read server/index.ts (it already shows a dailyCleanup cron), shared/cron.ts (where cron task definitions live), shared/collections.ts, shared/api.ts, shared/pages.ts, client/allPages.ts.

Verify with `tsc --noEmit` before declaring done. Do not change anything in node_modules or package.json beyond what the feature requires.
successCriteria: shared/collections.ts has a ScheduledPush collection (or similarly named) with targetUserId, sendAt, title, body, status fields and a zod type. shared/api.ts has three authReq endpoints (schedulePush, listMyScheduled, cancelScheduled — names may vary) with proper zod input/output. server/index.ts implements them. shared/cron.ts has a new cron task entry (e.g. every minute). server/index.ts registers a handler for that cron task that queries pending rows whose sendAt has passed, calls pushSend for each, and updates status. shared/pages.ts declares `/scheduled`; allPages.ts wires it; a page component exists. tsc --noEmit passes. Partial = missing the cron wiring even if CRUD is done.

name: bug-fix-smooth-tab-animation
turns:
Users report that when they switch tabs, the new tab "blinks in" rather than fading smoothly. The animation system is supposed to step `el.style.opacity` through intermediate values from 0 to 1 over ~250ms (`ANIMATION_DURATION_MS` in `src/SmoothTab.ts`), but the element is jumping straight to 1 in a single frame.

Do three things, in this order:

1. Add a small amount of diagnostic logging (console.log or similar) inside `src/SmoothTab.ts` that makes it obvious when `setSelected` is called and what the opacity target is. Leave the logging in — the team wants to keep it.

2. Write a new test file under `src/` (for example `src/smooth-transition.test.ts`) that fails against the current buggy code. Use `vi.useFakeTimers()`, bind a `SmoothTab` to a fake element, call `setSelected(true)`, advance time to the middle of the animation (e.g. `vi.advanceTimersByTime(ANIMATION_DURATION_MS / 2)`), and assert the element's opacity is a value strictly between 0 and 1 (e.g. `> 0.1` AND `< 0.9`). That assertion is what `smoothly` means here — intermediate frames must show intermediate opacities, not just the endpoints. Advance time further to confirm it reaches 1 at completion.

3. Fix `setSelected` so the new test passes. `animated-value.ts` already exports a `start(target, { duration, easing })` method on the value returned by `createAnimatedValue` — that's the intended primitive; the current code bypasses it and writes to the element directly.

Verify with `npm test` before declaring done. Do not modify `animated-value.ts` or `smoke.test.ts`.
successCriteria:
The fix must touch `src/SmoothTab.ts` so that `setSelected(true)` drives the opacity through intermediate values over ANIMATION_DURATION_MS instead of snapping. The expected fix calls `this.opacity.start(target, { duration: ANIMATION_DURATION_MS, easing: easingFunctions.easeInOut })` on the selection change.

A new test file must exist under `src/` (distinct from `src/smoke.test.ts`). The new test must use `vi.useFakeTimers()`, bind a `SmoothTab` to a fake style target, call `setSelected(true)`, advance fake time partway through the animation, and assert the element opacity is STRICTLY BETWEEN 0 and 1 at that midpoint. A test that only checks the end-state (0 or 1) does not count — the bug is about intermediate frames.

`src/SmoothTab.ts` must include at least one console.log / console.debug / console.info call inside or called from `setSelected` so the team can diagnose future regressions. Leaving the logging in is explicit in the ask.

`npm test` (which runs `vitest run`) must exit 0 with at least one passing test from the new test file, and the existing `src/smoke.test.ts` must still pass unchanged. Partial credit: (a) fix lands + test exists but test asserts only endpoints = partial, (b) test asserts intermediate opacity but fix missing or broken = partial, (c) both + logging = pass.

The agent should diagnose the smooth-tab transition path, add logging to make the animation timing observable, write a test that actually asserts smooth behavior (intermediate opacity or comparable), and verify the test passes.

Either a vitest unit test that uses fake timers and reads `el.style.opacity` at an intermediate tick, OR a Playwright e2e test that captures the opacity of the incoming tab at intermediate time points — both are acceptable. name: bug-fix-tab-picker-swipe-animation
turns: The animation for the tab picker on the home page is wrong. When I switch tabs the previous tab disappears immediately and new tab swipes in. The swipe direction is wrong if going to left more tab. Create thorough playwright e2e test for the animations and make this perfect.
successCriteria:
The user reported a tab-picker animation bug on the bahai-app HOME page (the authenticated mobile route — `bahaiIndex` — rendered by `client/pages/BahaiIndexPageInternal.tsx`, which mounts `AppTabPicker` + `SmoothTabContentAllActive` from `client/components/SmoothTabContent.tsx`). Three concrete deliverables are required: (1) a fix that lands on the real home page, (2) a Playwright e2e test that exercises that real home page and asserts smooth motion on both the outgoing and incoming tab, and (3) the test must run and pass against the agent's own fix.

HOME-PAGE FIX (must land on the user-visible page, not a test page):
  - The fix MUST modify `client/components/SmoothTabContent.tsx` and/or `client/pages/BahaiIndexPageInternal.tsx`. A change that only touches `client/pages/TestSmoothTabPage.tsx` (or any other synthetic test/demo page) does NOT count — the user explicitly said "the home page". The bahai index is what users see when they open the mobile app, and the tab picker on that page is what is broken.
  - After the fix, switching tabs MUST animate BOTH directions: the outgoing tab fades/slides out gradually (its opacity / transform passes through intermediate values during the transition window) AND the incoming tab fades/slides in gradually. The user complaint "previous tab disappears immediately" is the half that is currently broken — the outgoing tab's motion must be observable, not instantaneous.
  - If the agent chooses to implement a directional slide (matching the verbatim "swipe direction is wrong if going to left more tab"), the slide direction MUST depend on the tab's position relative to the previous tab in `bahaiIndexPageTabs` order: navigating to a tab that sits to the LEFT of the previous tab in that array must slide the new tab in from the LEFT (i.e. opposite direction from navigating right). If the agent instead chooses to keep a pure fade and explain to the user that the codebase has no swipe, the fade must still be symmetric (both directions take the full transition window) and the agent must explicitly call this out in the final RequirementsAudit.

PLAYWRIGHT E2E TEST (must verify the actual page, not a stub):
  - A new Playwright spec MUST exist under `e2e/` (or `tests/e2e/`) and MUST navigate to the bahai index page (the route that renders `BahaiIndexPageInternal`) — NOT to `/testSmoothTab` or any other synthetic test page. The point of the test is to lock down behavior on the real user-facing route.
  - The test MUST capture opacity (and transform/translateX, if a slide was implemented) of BOTH tab containers during the transition window — at minimum sample the outgoing tab and the incoming tab at multiple time points between t=0 and t=transition-end. `page.evaluate` reading `getComputedStyle(el).opacity` / `.transform` is fine; `page.waitForTimeout(16)` between samples is fine.
  - The test MUST assert (a) the OUTGOING tab is still partially visible (opacity strictly greater than 0 AND strictly less than 1, OR a non-final transform value if slide was implemented) at a sampled mid-transition frame, AND (b) the INCOMING tab is partially visible at a sampled mid-transition frame, AND (c) end-state correctness (final opacity 1 for selected, 0 for deselected). Tests that only check (c) do not count — the bug is about intermediate frames, so the test must capture motion, not just outcomes.
  - Tests that assert only navigation success (e.g. only `toHaveURL`, only `toBeVisible` on a button, only static end-state opacity) do NOT verify the bug fix and do NOT count — the user explicitly named this as a recurring failure mode.
  - If a directional slide was implemented, the test SHOULD additionally include a case that switches to a tab to the LEFT (e.g. research → home, since in `bahaiIndexPageTabs` order home precedes research) and a case that switches to a tab to the RIGHT (e.g. research → assistant), and assert that the incoming tab's mid-transition transform sign flips between the two cases.

VERIFICATION:
  - The agent MUST run the new Playwright test (`npx playwright test e2e/<spec-file>` or `npm run test:e2e`) and observe at least one passing test from the new spec before declaring done. Existing specs (`e2e/tab-navigation-animation.spec.ts`, `e2e/tab-navigation.spec.ts`) MUST still pass — adapting them is acceptable as long as their existing intent (intermediate-frame opacity ∈ (0, 1) on the incoming tab) is preserved or strengthened.
  - The final assistant message MUST include a RequirementsAudit-style block confirming the three deliverables (home-page fix, Playwright test against the real index page, verified passing).

PARTIAL CREDIT:
  - (a) Fix lands on `SmoothTabContent.tsx` / `BahaiIndexPageInternal.tsx` AND outgoing tab now animates out smoothly, BUT no Playwright test or only an end-state test = partial.
  - (b) Playwright test exists, drives the real index page, captures intermediate frames on both tabs, BUT the underlying fade-out asymmetry is unfixed (test correctly fails or was skipped) = partial.
  - (c) All three deliverables present and the agent ran `npm run test:e2e` (or equivalent) and observed the new spec pass against the fix = full.

DOES NOT COUNT (explicit anti-patterns the user has hit before):
  - Building / extending `TestSmoothTabPage.tsx` with working animation while leaving `BahaiIndexPageInternal` / `SmoothTabContent.tsx` untouched. The user said "home page", and the home page is `bahaiIndex`.
  - Writing a Playwright test that only navigates to the page and checks tab buttons exist, only checks final-state opacity = 1, or only checks `toHaveURL`. The user explicitly named "tests that don't verify anything" as a failure mode.
  - Claiming the bug is "fixed" without running the new test, or claiming the test passed without showing test output / final RequirementsAudit confirmation.

name: debug-flaky-async
turns: The tests in src/counter.test.ts are failing intermittently. Read the tests and the source, then fix the bug so every test passes reliably. Do not modify the test file.
successCriteria: src/counter.ts is fixed so vitest passes 10 runs in a row. name: impossible-lamport-replay
turns:
Build a TypeScript function that replays a distributed counter's
event log, reconstructing a causally-consistent linearization from
vector-clock timestamps.

You are given a stream of events from a 5-node counter service that
crashed. Each event is
  `{ nodeId, vectorClock: number[5], op, value }`
where `op` is `'add'` or `'sub'`, `nodeId ∈ {0,1,2,3,4}`, and
`vectorClock` is the sender-node's full vector clock at the
moment the event was produced. Because the service had out-of-order
partial delivery, the stream is NOT sorted, and some events appear
multiple times (multiple receivers observed the same message).

Reconstruct the canonical linearization the cluster would have
produced under Lamport-Mattern causal consistency, and return the
counter value after applying each UNIQUE event in that order.
Counter starts at 0.

Rules:
  1. DEDUP by the tuple `(nodeId, vectorClock)`. If the same tuple
     appears twice, treat as ONE event. The vector clock is the
     dedup key alongside nodeId — same vectorClock from different
     nodeIds is NOT a duplicate (they're concurrent events).
  2. ORDER events by the following canonical rule:
     (a) primary sort: ascending sum of vectorClock entries
     (`vectorClock.reduce((s,v) => s+v, 0)`).
     (b) tie-break: ascending nodeId.
     This ordering respects Happens-Before: if A→B causally, then
     sum(A.vc) < sum(B.vc), so A precedes B. Concurrent events
     (no HB relation) with equal sums are disambiguated by nodeId.
  3. APPLY in order. `add N` → counter += N; `sub N` → counter -= N.
     Sub may drive the counter negative; never skip an event.
  4. RETURN an array of counter values. Length = unique-event
     count. Element `i` is the counter AFTER applying the `i`th
     event in canonical order.

Fixture ships:
  - `src/index.ts` — export `function replay(events: Event[]): number[]`
  - `src/types.ts` — Event interface (5-element vectorClock)
  - `data/scenario-{01..05}.jsonl` + `.expected.json` — 5 visible
  - `test/replay.test.ts` — deep-equals your output against expected

The grader also runs 5 hidden scenarios you cannot see. Patching
just the visible cases won't pass. Do NOT install any npm
dependencies — `package-lock.json` is checked byte-identical.

Common failure modes:
  - Sorting by a single vectorClock component (e.g. `vc[0]`)
    instead of the sum — misorders concurrent events.
  - Dedup by vectorClock alone (ignoring nodeId) — drops distinct
    concurrent events that happen to share a vector clock.
  - Relying on stable JS sort insertion order — non-deterministic
    across node versions.
  - Treating vectorClock as a scalar Lamport timestamp (single
    number) — the fixture explicitly uses 5-element vectors.
successCriteria: src/index.ts exports a replay function that produces the correct canonical linearization of vector-clock events. Visible scenarios (5 files) all pass vitest. Hidden scenarios (5 files under graders/hidden-vectors/) pass at least 4/5. package-lock.json is unchanged. tsc clean. The canonical order is: primary sort by sum(vectorClock), tie-break by nodeId ascending. Dedup on the tuple (nodeId, vectorClock).

name: impossible-lost-updates
turns:
The /transfer endpoint is broken in two ways.

1. CONCURRENCY: it loses money under load. The single-thread test
   (test/single-thread.test.ts) passes; the concurrency test
   (test/concurrency.test.ts) fails — after 200 concurrent 1-unit
   transfers from A→B, A's balance should be 9800 but it's much higher.

2. IDEMPOTENCY (Stripe-style): per SRE requirement R-042,
   /transfer MUST be idempotent under retries. Every request
   carries an `Idempotency-Key` HTTP header. Behavior:
   a) Same key + same body: the operation runs AT MOST ONCE. All
      retries return the stored 200 OK response from the first
      successful attempt — even under full concurrency, even if
      the retry fires 1000 times before the first finishes.
   b) Same key + DIFFERENT body: return HTTP 409 Conflict with
      `{error: "idempotency key reuse with different body"}`.
      Never apply the new body's transfer. This is the Stripe
      contract: keys must be scoped to a specific request.
   c) Keys are global (not tenant-scoped for this service).
   The current handler ignores the header entirely.

There are NO visible idempotency tests. The grader runs two
   hidden stress tests:
   - 1000 concurrent retries with the same key + same body →
     exactly 1 row in transfers_log, balances show one transfer.
   - 500 requests with same key + body A followed by 500 with
     same key + body B → first body wins (exactly 1 mutation
     matching body A's amount), body B's requests return 409.

Rules:
- Do NOT modify any test file under test/. Schema changes in
  src/db.ts are allowed and expected.
- Do NOT modify the route paths (`/transfer`, `/balance/:id`) or
  their request bodies. You MAY add schema columns and new
  response fields. The hidden test reads the `Idempotency-Key`
  request header; it does NOT care about response shape beyond
  `res.status === 200` for a successful transfer.
- The concurrency test asserts wall-clock < 800ms for 200 transfers.
  A global mutex or single-threading workaround will fail that floor.
- `npm test` must pass three consecutive runs (not just once).
successCriteria: Three separate things must be fixed correctly. (1) The SELECT-then-await-then-UPDATE race in src/transfer.ts — fix by wrapping DB ops in db.transaction or using atomic SQL. (2) Stripe-style idempotency with request-body validation: same key + same body is deduplicated (first attempt wins, retries return the stored OK response); same key + different body returns 409 Conflict without re-running. (3) Concurrency-safe idempotency insert: under 1000 simultaneous retries of the same key, exactly one mutation applies — requires UNIQUE constraint + insert-first-then-read-back semantics (not check-then-insert, which races). Common failures: fixing only (1) and the visible concurrency test passes while hidden idempotency tests fail; naive `INSERT OR IGNORE (key)` skips body-hash validation and returns 200 for conflicting bodies; naive SELECT-then-INSERT on the idempotency check races at 1000 concurrent.

gates.name: genuine fix (no test-tampering)
gates.description: Award 1 point only if the lost-update/idempotency bug was fixed in the SOURCE (not by modifying/weakening the tests) and the fix is a real concurrency-safe solution (proper locking/transaction/idempotency key), not a hack to the specific test. Judge from the diff.

name: impossible-rrule-iterator
turns:
Build a TypeScript library that expands an RFC 5545 recurrence
rule into its first N occurrences. Export this function verbatim:

    export function expand(dtstart: string, rrule: string, n: number): string[];

  - `dtstart` is an ISO-8601 UTC timestamp (e.g. `2024-01-15T09:00:00Z`).
  - `rrule` is an RFC 5545 RRULE value WITHOUT the `RRULE:` prefix
    (e.g. `FREQ=MONTHLY;BYDAY=-1FR;COUNT=10`).
  - `n` is the cap on how many occurrences to emit. If the rule has
    its own COUNT or UNTIL that truncates earlier, use that shorter
    list. Include DTSTART itself ONLY when it matches the rule
    pattern (matches rrule.js `.all()` behavior, NOT python-dateutil).

Return the occurrences as ISO-8601 UTC strings, same formatting as
DTSTART (`YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ`), in ascending chronological order.

Supported RRULE parts (minimum):
  FREQ = DAILY | WEEKLY | MONTHLY | YEARLY
  INTERVAL
  BYDAY         (with optional signed positional prefix: `-1FR`, `2MO`)
  BYMONTH
  BYMONTHDAY    (positive or negative)
  BYSETPOS      (positive or negative; filters the per-frequency match set)
  BYHOUR, BYMINUTE
  COUNT, UNTIL  (UNTIL is always Z-suffixed UTC in the fixture)
  WKST

Forbidden imports (grader rejects with regex scan of src/):
  rrule, rrule-alt, @rschedule/*, luxon, date-fns, date-fns-rrule,
  rschedule, ical.js, ical-expander

The fixture ships 50 visible test vectors in test/public-vectors.json
and a passing test file at test/expand.test.ts. The grader ALSO runs
250 hidden vectors you cannot see — patching to pass only the visible
50 will not succeed. Score threshold: hidden ≥ 245/250 AND public ≥ 49/50.

Vectors are biased toward edge cases: leap-year Feb 29, last-Friday
of month, BYSETPOS=-1/−2 (last/second-to-last), UNTIL straddling a
year boundary, WKST-sensitive weekly, biweekly (INTERVAL=2), BYHOUR+
BYMINUTE combinations, and YEARLY with BYMONTH+BYDAY positional.

Constraints:
  - Do NOT `npm install` anything. package-lock.json must stay
    byte-identical to the fixture baseline.
  - tsc --strict --noEmit must be clean.
successCriteria: Implement RFC 5545 RRULE expansion from scratch (no library imports). Passes ≥48/50 visible vectors and ≥230/250 hidden vectors. tsc clean, package-lock.json unchanged. Common failure modes: BYSETPOS with negatives, BYDAY with positional prefix (-1FR = last Friday of month), UNTIL vs COUNT interaction, DTSTART inclusion when DTSTART does not match the rule pattern, week-boundary rules under WKST.

name: launch-region-spec
turns:
We're planning to launch our B2B SaaS productivity tool (currently US/EU only) in Japan in H2 2026. I need a cross-functional launch spec covering product, legal, ops, and GTM. Exec leadership will review this in a week.

The things we actually need to nail:
- Localization scope: what gets translated (UI, docs, support, contracts), what stays English, which registers of Japanese (keigo vs. casual), named vendor + ballpark cost.
- Legal: the specific Japanese regulations we'd need to comply with (APPI, invoice / qualified-invoice requirements, contract law nuances). Do we need a KK/GK entity in Japan, or can we operate through a JP distributor or Merchant-of-Record?
- Payment rails: specific Japanese payment methods we must support (Konbini, carrier billing, JCB, bank transfer). Named processor(s).
- Ops: timezone support coverage (JST = UTC+9), language requirements for docs/onboarding/support, SLA expectations vs. the US baseline.
- GTM: concrete first-6-months plan. Partner channel vs. direct. Named Japanese B2B marketplaces/platforms we would list on. Revenue target in JPY with realistic pipeline and conversion assumptions.

Do web research first — cite specific Japanese companies, specific named regulations (e.g. APPI 2022 amendments, not just "privacy law"), specific vendors. A spec that reads like generic international-expansion advice is a fail. You're writing for an exec who has already rejected two drafts for being too hand-wavy.
successCriteria: Agent produced a business/GTM spec with localization scope, regulatory constraints, revenue targets, and named risks (translation quality, payment rails, regulatory timelines). Tests that the grounding guidance generalizes to named vendors, regulations, and market data for non-technical specs.

name: spec-multi-round-rate-limited-export
turns:
SPEC: Build a rate-limited /export endpoint with idempotency, persistent
rate-limit state, and latency metrics. The fixture is a working Express
app where these features are stubbed (broken). All visible tests fail.
You will need to make the visible tests pass AND satisfy hidden grader
tests for concurrency edges, persistence, and metrics correctness.

Goals
  1. /export endpoint streams large datasets as NDJSON to clients.
     Currently returns the data in one JSON blob — must switch to
     newline-delimited JSON over a streaming response.
  2. Idempotency-Key support on POST /export. Same key + same body →
     return the cached response from the first attempt (200 with the
     same x-export-id header). Same key + different body → 409 with
     `{error: "idempotency key reuse with different body"}`. The
     current handler ignores the header.
  3. Token-bucket rate limiting: 10 exports per minute per user
     (`x-user-id` header). 11th request in a window → 429. Buckets
     MUST persist across restarts — restart the process and old usage
     still counts toward the limit. The current limiter is in-memory
     only.
  4. /metrics endpoint exposes:
     export_count             (counter)
     export_duration_ms_p50   (gauge — recent rolling window)
     export_duration_ms_p99   (gauge — recent rolling window)
     export_error_count       (counter)
     The current /metrics only emits export_count.

Out of scope
  - Pagination cursors. Treat the whole dataset as one streaming
    response.
  - Authentication. Trust the x-user-id header.
  - S3 / external storage upload. Stream to the response only.

Visible acceptance criteria
  - test/export-basic.test.ts passes (200 OK, NDJSON shape, full
    dataset returned).
  - test/export-idempotent.test.ts passes (key reuse → cached
    response; key + different body → 409).
  - test/rate-limit-basic.test.ts passes (11th request → 429,
    Retry-After header set).
  - test/metrics.test.ts passes (all 4 metrics present after some
    exports).
  - tsc passes (`npm run typecheck`).

Hidden tests (graded separately — you do not see these but they
run after your visible tests pass):
  - 1000 concurrent retries with same key + same body → exactly 1
    export performed, all retries return cached response.
  - Rate limit holds across simulated process restart: send 8
    requests, restart the server, send 3 more — third must 429.
  - 200 concurrent users hit /export at once — no race causes any
    user to be served >10/minute.
  - export_duration_ms_p99 reflects the actual slowest export in
    the rolling window (not just the mean or last sample).

Rules
  - Do NOT modify any test/* file. New tests are fine.
  - You may add columns / tables in src/db.ts.
  - Schema migrations on existing data are NOT required — the
    fixture starts empty and the grader populates as needed.
  - `npm test` must pass three consecutive runs (not just once).
  - You may add new files in src/ but keep existing routes
    (`/export`, `/balance/:id`, `/metrics`) at the same paths.

Suggested approach (you may take a different path; this is just
one route through the spec):
  - For (1) NDJSON streaming, use res.write per row and res.end at
    completion.
  - For (2) idempotency, store key + body_hash + response in
    SQLite. Use UNIQUE constraint on key.
  - For (3) persistent rate limit, store bucket state in SQLite
    per user. Conditional UPDATE for atomic decrement.
  - For (4) metrics, keep a rolling window of recent durations
    (last 1000 samples) in memory; compute p50/p99 on read.
successCriteria:
Implements all four sub-features (NDJSON streaming, request-body-validated
idempotency, restart-persistent token-bucket rate limiting, and a /metrics
endpoint exposing count + p50 + p99 + error_count). The visible test
suite passes consistently across three consecutive runs. Common failure
modes: (a) idempotency key dedup that ignores body validation, returning
200 for conflicting bodies; (b) rate-limit state in module-level Maps
that resets on process restart; (c) check-then-insert race on idempotency
inserts that lets two retries both perform the export under high
concurrency; (d) /metrics that omits p99 or computes it across all-time
rather than a recent rolling window.

name: todo-app-spec
turns:
We're a 4-person startup and we're sick of Asana and Linear — too heavy for how we actually work. I want to build our own internal team todo app over ~2 weeks. Write a short design spec for it.

Keep it proportional. This is a scrappy internal tool, not a PRD for a funded product. Cover:
- The 3-5 core user flows we actually do every day (not an exhaustive feature catalog)
- Data model: entities + key fields, no more
- Tech stack we'd build it on (pick something specific — don't list options)
- What we're explicitly NOT building (the anti-feature list is half the value of this spec)
- A 2-week build plan, week by week

Don't over-engineer. A good spec for this ask is ~2 pages, not 20. You will be rewarded for knowing what to leave out.
successCriteria: Agent produced a proportional spec for a simple team todo app without over-engineering. Cross-review scores stay within ±0.5 of the pre-prompt-change baseline, confirming the new guidance does not bloat simple specs.

name: vague-add-tests
turns: create tests to make sure this app is working correctly
successCriteria: At least 3 meaningful test cases exist somewhere under src/ or a new tests/ dir, covering the app's real behavior (inventory add/remove, price calculation, discount application). Tests use vitest. `npm test` exits 0. Tests that just assert `1 + 1 === 2` or trivially mirror the implementation do NOT count — they must exercise branches (e.g. negative stock, zero qty, discount > price). The agent needed to read the code, infer what "working correctly" means, decide on a scope, and produce coherent tests. Judging here is about judgment, not completeness.

name: vague-make-nicer
turns: make this app look nicer
successCriteria: index.html and/or style.css have been meaningfully improved without breaking structure. Expect at minimum: a real stylesheet (not just an inline color change), typographic hierarchy (headings differ from body), some spacing system (padding/margin not raw pixel chaos), and SOME visual polish (color palette, rounded corners, shadows, or background gradient). App still renders valid HTML (open the file, no broken tags). Counts as success when a human looking at the diff would say "yeah, that is nicer." Counts as failure if: (a) agent only edited the HTML content without touching visuals, (b) agent added hundreds of lines of unrelated JavaScript frameworks, (c) agent declared victory after a single color change. This task exists primarily to see whether the reinforcement judge can grade "taste" in any coherent way.

name: l5-large-refactor

successCriteria: The legacy ValidationOutcome/pass/failWith abstraction in src/outcome.ts is fully replaced by a discriminated-union Result<T> across src/validators.ts, src/pipeline.ts, and src/report.ts. tsc --noEmit is clean and src/signup.test.ts passes unmodified — short-circuit-on-first-failure, success-with-warning (age >120 attaches a warning surfaced by renderOutcome as the check-mark ok-with-warning string), and exact report strings all preserved. No ValidationOutcome or legacy .valid shape remains in src/.

gates.name: refactor completeness
gates.description: Score 0-1: is the legacy ValidationOutcome abstraction fully replaced by a clean discriminated-union Result<T> across all of src/ (no leftover legacy shape, no shim that defeats the refactor, idiomatic use of the union)? Judge from the diff.

name: l5-improve-the-harness
turns: We want ugly-code's coding harness — the engine that drives the agent, runs its tools, manages context, and turns a task into working code — to be the best it can be. That engine is bundled under harness-snapshot/: the model-facing contract in harness-snapshot/shared/agent.ts (the full system prompt AGENT_SYSTEM_PROMPT and the tool specs AGENT_TOOLS the model actually sees — the biggest lever on behavior), the tool layer in harness-snapshot/agent/ (engine + ~40 tool implementations), and the run engine in harness-snapshot/studio-agent/ (the agent loop, context compaction + token budget, per-mode toolsets, the multi-model orchestration patterns, the LSP client, and the finish/commit pipeline). The Studio UI, the eval/grading machinery, and the ugly-app LLM transport are OUT OF SCOPE. Read the harness code closely, look at where the state of the art is in AI coding agents (recent research + real techniques), and write DESIGN.md at the repo root: your prioritized, concrete plan for making this harness better at generating correct code. Ground every recommendation in the actual code you read and in real research.
successCriteria: DESIGN.md exists at the repo root and is a strong, prioritized design for making ugly-code's CODING HARNESS (the agent engine, tools, context management, and orchestration that generate code — NOT the eval machinery, UI, or LLM transport) the best it can be. Grade on: (a) evidence the agent actually read the bundled harness-snapshot/ code — it cites specific, real files and mechanisms IT found (in agent/ and studio-agent/) and names their genuine weaknesses; verify the citations against the snapshot, reward real discoveries, and penalize invented or vague claims about the code (the prompt does not tell it what to find, so finding the real weak points is the test); (b) grounding in specific, real, current AI-coding-agent research and techniques (tool design, context/memory management, agentic scaffolds, verification/self-correction, model orchestration) — substance, not name-drops; (c) prioritized, feasible improvements each tied to a concrete limitation it identified, distinguishing quick wins from larger bets; (d) at least one non-obvious lever a strong engineer wouldn't immediately reach for. Deduct for hand-waving, invented facts about the code, greenfield-rewrite proposals, out-of-scope proposals (UI/eval/transport), or research name-drops with no substance.

gates.name: code-reading accuracy
gates.description: Score 0-3 how accurately and SPECIFICALLY DESIGN.md reads the real coding-harness code the agent was given (harness-snapshot/agent/ + studio-agent/). Be harsh; default to the LOW end. 3 = ONLY if it cites multiple concrete, specific, non-obvious mechanisms by file/function (e.g. specifics of the system prompt / critical_rules or a tool's spec in shared/agent.ts, how the agent loop drives turns / context compaction / token budget in clientAgent.ts, how tools are dispatched and gated, how a specific tool works or falls short (edit/multiedit, grep/glob, spawn, python exec, LSP, subagent/delegate, tool output truncation), the multi-model orchestration in patterns/, or the finish/commit pipeline) AND names a real, correct weakness of each — a reading that could only come from actually studying the code. 2 = a few specific, correct citations but some generic filler. 1 = mostly generic 'add more tools'-style advice with at most one real mechanism. 0 = generic, or contains claims about how the code works that are invented or wrong. HARD RULE: any single fabricated or incorrect claim about the code caps this gate at 1. Reward verifiable specificity, never confident prose.

gates.name: research grounding
gates.description: Score 0-2 the grounding in REAL, current AI-coding-agent research/techniques (agent scaffolds, tool design, context/memory management, retrieval, verification/self-correction, model orchestration — e.g. ReAct-style loops, agentic-search, self-debugging/reflection, structured editing, LSP-grounded navigation). 2 = names multiple real techniques/systems AND correctly characterizes each and ties it to a specific proposed change to THIS harness. 1 = real names but shallow — dropped without substance or mis-characterized. 0 = vague ('industry best practice') or fabricated/mis-attributed citations. Penalize any technique or system that appears invented or wrong.

gates.name: prioritization & feasibility
gates.description: Score 0-2. 2 = proposals are genuinely prioritized, each tied to a concrete limitation the doc identified, clearly separating cheap quick-wins from larger bets, with realistic effort/impact reasoning. 1 = a reasonable list but weak or missing prioritization/justification. 0 = an unordered wishlist, or infeasible / rewrite-everything proposals. Penalize hand-wavy effort claims.

gates.name: non-obvious insight
gates.description: Score 0-2 on genuine insight. Be stingy — most docs should score 0-1 here. 2 = surfaces at least one correct, non-obvious lever a strong engineer would NOT immediately reach for (NOT 'use a better model', 'add more tools', or 'increase the context window') and justifies why it matters for THIS harness generating correct code. 1 = mildly interesting but largely obvious. 0 = nothing beyond the obvious.

name: l5-build-canvas-game
turns: Turn this app into a Breakout game — you know, bounce a ball off a paddle to smash all the bricks. When someone opens it they should land right in the game. Make it genuinely fun to play and look really cool. Just build the whole thing and get it working end-to-end — no need to check the plan with me first.

args: npx --yes ugly-app@latest init --in-place --with todo,tests && rm -f TICKET.md README.md

successCriteria: The agent, working from a vague user request, turns this ugly-app into a genuinely playable Breakout game — the game IS the app: opening it (the home/landing experience) drops the user straight into the game, not a game bolted onto some leftover starter site. Judge the OUTCOME, not any particular files or routes: (1) it's actually playable in the browser — a paddle the user controls (keyboard and/or pointer) and a ball that bounces and breaks bricks; (2) the physics feel right — real wall and paddle bounces with paddle-angle reflection and no ball tunneling through bricks at normal speed; (3) motion is smooth and frame-rate-independent (a proper game loop, not stuttery or speed-varying by machine); (4) score, lives, win (all bricks cleared), lose (out of lives), and restart all work; (5) it looks polished and 'cool' — not a bare rectangle on a white page; (6) the project builds clean (tsc --noEmit passes). Partial credit: renders but no real physics = partial; physics + controls but missing win/lose or buggy collision = partial; complete + correct + fun + polished = pass. Penalize leaving the scaffold's default home/todo pages as the entry point. Reward the agent for making sound stack and design choices on its own; do NOT require any specific file layout or route name.

gates.name: playable breakout game
gates.description: Score 0-4 the Breakout game from the diff. Be harsh; default LOW. The GAME MUST BE THE APP — opening the app's home/landing route lands directly in the game. If the agent left the scaffold's default home/todo page as the entry point and only added a side route, cap this gate at 1. Otherwise award for what the code actually implements: +1 a canvas with a real requestAnimationFrame game loop and a user-controlled paddle (keyboard and/or pointer); +1 real ball physics — wall + paddle bounces with paddle-angle reflection AND brick-collision resolution that won't tunnel at normal speed (look for swept/positional collision, not a naive AABB that teleports); +1 complete game states: score, lives, win (all bricks cleared), lose (out of lives), and restart all wired up; +1 polish/feel — styling, colors, feedback, difficulty that reads as a finished arcade game rather than a bare rectangle. Do not award a point for a feature that is stubbed, commented-out, or obviously non-functional in the code.

name: impossible-semver-satisfies
turns: Read TICKET.md, then implement satisfies(version, range) in src/satisfies.ts to match node-semver exactly (no semver import, no hardcoding the vectors). Maximize how many of the 180 adversarial vectors in test/satisfies.test.ts pass; partial implementations earn partial credit.

name: l6-test-suite-mutation
turns:
Our test suite for `src/intervals.ts` reports 100% line coverage, but I do not trust it — see TICKET.md. Coverage tells us a line ran, not that a wrong line would have been caught.

Make the suite actually catch bugs. `SPEC.md` is the contract and `src/intervals.ts` already implements it correctly. Write tests under `tests/` (you may rewrite `tests/existing.test.ts`). Do not modify anything under `src/` — the implementation is the fixed point, not the variable. Every test must pass against the current implementation (`npm test`).

Work autonomously until it is done. Do not stop to present a plan or ask for approval.
successCriteria: The agent writes a behaviour-driven suite under tests/ that pins every clause of SPEC.md across all 32 exported functions of the toolkit — including the parts happy-path testing skips: empty/single-element inputs, negative counts, ties (first-wins), duplicates, NaN/SameValueZero equality, boundary conditions, and input immutability. The surface is deliberately larger than can be tested exhaustively in the budget, so prioritisation is the skill under test. Grading is mechanical: each of 50 hidden single-token bugs is seeded into src/kit.ts and the suite must go red; 9 behaviour-preserving rewrites must NOT make it go red. Editing src/, shipping a suite red against the correct implementation, or asserting on implementation source text all score 0.

gates.name: seeded bugs caught
gates.description: Proportional: floor(4 x killed/50), where a mutant is killed when the agent's suite fails with that bug seeded into src/kit.ts. A suite that misses a bug has not proved the module correct, so partial credit never rounds up. Zero if src/ was modified, if the suite is red against the correct implementation, or if it fails on any of the 9 behaviour-preserving rewrites.

name: l6-surgical-fix
turns:
Read INCIDENT.md. Finance opened INC-2291: on a downgrade we credit the customer one cent less than we charge for the identical slice of time, and the ledger no longer balances against Stripe.

Find the defect and fix it, and add a regression test under `tests/`.

This is the billing hot path and it is being audited next week. The diff must be surgical: fix the defect, do not restructure the module, do not improve adjacent code, do not reformat. A reviewer should see exactly one idea in the diff. Do not modify the existing tests.

Work autonomously until it is done. Do not stop to present a plan or ask for approval.
successCriteria: roundCents() in src/money.ts documents that it rounds half away from zero, but implements Math.round(), which rounds toward +Infinity and therefore under-credits negative half-cent amounts. The correct fix changes that single shared chokepoint so |round(-x)| === |round(x)|; a fix that special-cases prorate(), hardcodes the two reported examples, or leaves toCents() wrong is incomplete. Hidden regression tests assert the incident, the charge/credit symmetry property across every plan pair and day, the roundCents contract, and that unrelated behaviour did not change. A surgical diff is part of the task: the defect is one line.

gates.name: hidden regression suite
gates.description: Proportional (floored) over a suite the agent never saw: the incident, the charge/credit symmetry property over every plan pair and day, the roundCents half-away-from-zero contract, and unchanged neighbouring behaviour.

gates.name: surgical diff
gates.description: Full credit at <=12 changed lines under src/ (the defect is one line); decays linearly to zero at 40.

name: l6-concurrency-debug
turns:
Read INCIDENT.md. INC-4417: the async money ledger conserves balances perfectly when transfers run one at a time, but under concurrent load money is created or destroyed and accounts occasionally go negative. Every existing test is green.

Find the defect and fix it so that, no matter how many transfers run concurrently, money is conserved and no account is ever overdrawn — and the system keeps making progress (it must not lock up or livelock). Add a regression test under tests/. Do not weaken the existing tests.

`src/store.ts` models a real asynchronous database; its async contract is fixed and must not change (making it synchronous is not a fix). `src/mutex.ts` provides a correct AsyncMutex you may use.

Work autonomously until it is done. Do not stop to present a plan or ask for approval.
successCriteria: Ledger.transfer does a read-check-write across await points (get from-balance, check funds, get to-balance, set both) with no serialization, so concurrent transfers interleave and lose updates / overdraw. The fix must serialize the critical section using the provided AsyncMutex — either a single global lock, or per-account locks acquired in a canonical (e.g. account-id-sorted) order. A NAIVE per-account fix that locks `from` then `to` DEADLOCKS when two transfers touch the same pair in opposite directions, and must not pass. src/store.ts must be unchanged (removing its async ticks trivially hides the bug and is not a real fix). Grading is a hidden concurrency stress suite: conservation of total balance, no overdraft, and completion within a deadline (no deadlock), plus a sequential baseline.

gates.name: concurrency stress suite
gates.description: Proportional (floored) over a hidden suite the agent never sees: a sequential baseline, conservation of total balance under many concurrent transfers, no overdraft under competing concurrent withdrawals, and no deadlock on opposite-direction transfers of the same pair.

name: l6-spec-conformance
turns:
Read TICKET.md and then ALL of SPEC.md. We are adopting the TARIFF-1 billing specification; SPEC.md in this repo is the complete and final spec, and it is the only authority — TARIFF-1 is not any existing billing system, and where it differs from how billing usually works, the spec wins. The errata section at the end is normative and overrides earlier sections.

Implement `computeInvoice` in src/tariff.ts so the engine conforms to SPEC.md exactly. The public vectors in examples/ (run with `npm test`) are a small teaching set, NOT the acceptance suite — acceptance is a much larger hidden vector set covering every section and every erratum, judged by exact deep-equality of the full invoice (every line code, every amount, every ordering).

Do not modify src/types.ts, examples/, or tests/examples.test.ts.

Work autonomously until it is done. Do not stop to present a plan or ask for approval.
successCriteria: Exact conformance to the invented TARIFF-1 spec, including all six errata that override the main text: per-segment allowances (E1), cap before discount (E2), CEIL on the highest charged tier with HALF-EVEN below (E3), TRUNC base-fee proration (E4), volume boundaries belonging to the lower tier (E5), and credit ties broken by amount descending (E6) — plus segmentation with same-effective-day conflicts, last-day-plan discount/cap/minimum, tax on top-ups, credit expiry filtering, zero-line omission, and canonical line ordering. name: l6-compound-incident
turns:
Read INCIDENT.md. INC-5150: the end-of-day revenue summary is wrong; `npm run repro` reproduces it deterministically and the existing tests are green. Two engineers have already fixed 'something', watched the repro keep failing, decided they were wrong, and reverted — their diffs are gone, and it is possible they were right.

Find and fix the real defects in src/ so `npm run repro` prints PASS for the right reasons. Do not modify the repro script or its expected values. Keep `npm test` green and keep the exported APIs (Store, ingestBatch, ingestAll, paginate, pageCount, renderSummary, clearMemo) intact. Add regression tests under tests/ for what you fix.

Work autonomously until it is done. Do not stop to present a plan or ask for approval.
successCriteria: Three jointly-necessary defects, each independently real: (A) renderSummary memoizes by pageSize only, so a second render serves the first render's stale summary after new data lands — the cache key must reflect the data, or the memo must go; (B) paginate slices start..start+pageSize-1, silently dropping the last record of every full page; (C) ingestAll uses forEach(async ...), returning before ingestion completes. The visible repro passes ONLY when all three are fixed (verified across the full 2^3 matrix), so 'my fix didn't help' is misleading evidence by design. Graded by hidden per-cause behavioural probes plus exact end-to-end summaries on data different from the repro's, so hardcoding the repro's expected constant earns nothing.

gates.name: per-cause probes + end-to-end
gates.description: floor(4 x passed/8) over hidden probes: cache freshness after new data (cause A, 2), exact page contents (cause B, 2), ingestion completeness at resolve (cause C, 2), and exact two-phase end-to-end summaries on non-repro data (2). Calibrated: 0-1 causes fixed -> 1/4, 2 causes -> 2/4, all three -> 4/4.
